speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six, September 17th, 1996. Interviewee is General Felix Sparks, interviewer Tony [INAUDIBLE], being done in Lakewood, Colorado, United States of America, and it's being done in English. General Sparks, you were telling us about your children and some of your grandchildren. You said your children live all around Denver.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, not all of them. I have one granddaughter who lives in Milwaukee-- --and one grandson who's now-- whose in the Army. But he'll be home this month and starting college right away. All of my four children served in the military, but none of them cared for it very much. They all spent one enlistment-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --either in the Army or in the Navy. Two of my grandsons, one spent enlistment in the Air Force. He just graduated college this year. The other one is in the Army. He'll be getting out this month. He completes his four years and he'll be starting college. So practically all of my-- all of my kids and part of my grandchildren have had some type of military service in either the Army, Navy, or Air Force. But none of them cared for it and I didn't care for it, either, as a steady diet. Although I spent eight years on active duty, I didn't want to make a career out of it, although I practically did because I got called back months after the end of World War II. When I got so busy with my civilian occupation, I-- I was a director of natural resources for the state of Colorado for a while, the director of the State Water Conservation Board. And so I was always busy, in-- in addition to my National Guard duties. So I had time to-- well, I had to-- to get promoted to brigadier general, I had to attend the Army War College at Fort Leavenworth, Kansas. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, it's called the Army Command and General Staff College at Fort Leavenworth; the Army War College at Carlisle, Pennsylvania; and the National War College at Fort McNair, Washington, DC. So I've been a part time soldier or full time soldier and almost all my life-- --and racked up a total of over 15 years of what was called active duty. Since I retired from both the military in '77 and civilian job in '79m my wife and I decided that we were pretty well off financially and we decided we'd see the world. So we have traveled extensively throughout Europe and the Pacific since I retired until a little over three years ago, we got the worst shock of our lives. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our 16-- one of our grad 16-year-old grandson, who was a twin, twin boys, was killed in a drive-by shooting by some kid that he didn't even know. He wasn't the intended target, but he's the one who died. Well, the kid that killed him was 15 years old. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that time, we had no law whatsoever regarding juveniles and handguns. A kid 10 years old could pack a gun around, and they were doing it. We were having-- in the particular summer that my grandson was killed, there were something like 20 juveniles who were killed by handguns. So I helped them-- I decided I wasn't going to sit around and grieve about my grandson-- --that I would do something about it. So I got together with some influential people here in Colorado-- the district attorney's office in Denver and some others-- and we formed an organization called PUNCH, Parents United No Children's Handguns. And we met with the governor and he agreed to call a special session of the legislature that year to consider making it a crime for juveniles under the age of 18 to be carrying handguns. The special session was called in September of 1993, six months after my grandson was killed, and the legislature adopted a-- rather one of the toughest measures in the United States on juveniles and handguns. But our organization, PUNCH, we raised about $50,000 and put on a campaign, a statewide campaign, to influence legislators to enact that legislation. My wife and I put in about half of that, about-- about $25,000-- that we spent on that effort and we're continuing that effort today as far as juveniles and handguns are concerned. We are, of course, always opposed by the National Rifle Association, who believes that everybody should be free to kill everybody, as far as I can understand their philosophy. But despite that it's a very formidable organization and has a good many million dollars year in revenues, we were able here in Colorado to adopt a rather strict law. And since that time, over 200 kids have been picked up and charged with carrying handguns, very often in the schools. So we're making some headway, but the way society is today who knows. Money talks big and the gun industry is a billion dollar a year industry. The people making money from that source don't like to have any impediment put in the way of their selling guns-- to who, they don't care as long as they can sell guns. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, it took a lot of our energy away. We have not-- and a lot of our money and time-- and we have not traveled hardly any since that day. We do no more overseas travel. All we do is we get together with our annual reunion every year. I arrange for that and conduct it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But so life has its ups and down. Every family has tragedies and ours has had a great tragedy. We found that other families have experienced the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking that your 15 years of military service. You said you were called back into the service after World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that campaign or what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Cuba [INAUDIBLE]. I was called back as a [? strat ?] commander. At that time, I was a full colonel. And I was one-- assigned to the forces that were selected to make the invasion of Cuba, which as you know when Khrushchev backed down and agreed to take the missiles out of Cuba, that thing ended so I was only on for a year. But it did take a year out that I had not expected.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you also in the Korean?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Strangely enough, the unit I was with at that time was not called for the Korean duty. I was with an infantry regiment, but it was not called. Although other troops from the Colorado National Guard were called. Our air wing and medical battalion, tank battalion were all called to Korea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you made any trips? You said you and your wife went and did a lot of traveling. Did you ever go back to Germany or to Dachau?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back to Germany about five-- five times since the end of the war. Three of those trips included a visit to Dachau. I met with some survivors, German survivors, who were in Dachau. When arrived, they were political prisoners. And I've gone through their museum there about three times since the end of the war. The present German government has maintained a small museum at Dachau and every time I have been there, I've been surprised at the great number of tourists who go through that grim facility on a daily basis. Now, practically everything has been torn down now, but they have maintained two or three of those barracks, but they have been rebuilt. But they have exhibits, the pictures, some of the torturers, and they have a movie which they show in the museum which is in the old prison area itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the year inmates, that part?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, the outside is no longer open to the public, just the-- where the prison area was is-- is open to the public. The-- well, the crematoriums are still there. They're just outside of the prison area. But you can go through the crematoriums and the other buildings that were used to pile on the dead people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the museum itself is maintained by the German government at this time. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] A number of my men have been back there are also, men that I served with. On two occasions, we took-- we took members of our regimental association and their wives. At the time, I was by myself, but my wife has been there twice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we have visited Germany at least five times in our travels of Europe and we've been to most of the European countries, except the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --part that was under Russian domination. We've never been to any of those. My last visit to Dachau was just three years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a group here headed by a lieutenant governor, a small group of people, wanted to explore-- we were having a problem with neo-Nazis here in this country, as you know, and people who put out false propaganda. So a small group of us headed by our lieutenant governor and several Jewish people, also one Arabic type, Japanese, Chinese, we had an ethnic mix. We wanted to explore what the German government was doing about their neo-Nazi movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a chance to meet with German officials and with the American embassy in Bonn, Germany and-- and then go on to Munich and go through the museum at Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very rewarding visit, I think. We learned a lot about the problems that the Germans are having today. And they're having a neo-Nazi movement. We have the same thing here in the United States. What caught our attention was that in Holocaust memorial services that we have every year, we were going to have people picket with signs saying this is all a hoax, the Holocaust was a hoax. And then more disturbing to me was a few years ago-- --about two years ago, I was speaking in San Diego as a guest of a Marine base at Camp Pendleton, California, and one of the people in the audience, since the public was invited, one of the people in the audience came up to me and showed me a leaflet, I have copies of it here, which anyway was put out by an outfit called-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --the Historical Institute of Costa Mesa, California. And they put out a pamphlet which says-- has a bunch of questions and answers, which in effect states that the Holocaust never happened. An in that literature, it says that no Jews were killed in concentration camps, that was all made up and that the crematoriums in the concentration camps that still exist over there were constructed after the war as tourist attractions. Well, you have to be sick, very sick, to go out and preach today that the Holocaust never happened. And these are people with great hatreds. Obviously, they are a neo-Nazi type or Hitler worshippers. So I have done my best to try to dispel the idea that it never happened. And not only me, but there were thousands of American soldiers, British soldiers, Russian soldiers. Dachau was one of hundreds of concentration camps that the Germans maintained. So there were thousands of British, American, and Russian soldiers who liberated those camps who saw firsthand and thousands and thousands of pictures were taken. To say that it never happened is the height of stupidity and viciousness. So I'll fight those type of people till my last breath. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have great sympathy for the Jewish people who I have no question that something like six million died in concentration camps. But there are also, in addition to the Jewish people, there were others who died, as well. The inmates at Dachau when we arrived, actually, the bulk of them were Russians, Russian civilians. And the next biggest group was Polish and the next biggest group was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know how many numbers there were of each?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We took an almond joy of the number of people by races and we have that-- I have not inventory. Almost 32,000. But there were 25 or 30 different races represented. There were some Italians, there were French, Lithuanians-- you name it. And some Americans, but they were naturalized Americans who so apparently were caught back when they went back to visit their home areas. I think there were five or six that were naturalized Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see or talk with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never talked-- I talked with one. He was-- he was actually an American soldier who had-- was a member of the OSS, which at that time was our equivalent of a-- agency, the spy agency, the CIA. And he had been dropped into Germany of some kind of mission and been captured. And he told me that he'd only been to a short time. And he was the only actually American that was in there. He was a lieutenant, an American lieutenant. I talked to him very briefly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a French name and he spoke French and German. He was from Chicago. I have been able to trace him down since then, but he's died. I can't remember. He had a French name, I can't remember it offhand. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I'm not sure about his story, either. Because when I first-- when I first-- he first came to my attention was right after we arrived. He came up to the gate and was shouting, I'm an American soldier. So I said to the guards, let him out. He came out and he told me he was a major-- oh, something like [? Girole ?] was his name, something like that. Rene [? Girole. ?] And he told me he was an American major in OSS. Well, I thought he was. But I found out later, his testimony is in here, in this book, and now he turns have to be a first lieutenant, not a major at all. So I don't-- I'm not sure about his story, how he got there. But his testimony is in this book that I have. Some peculiar things happened there. That-- that was always a mystery to me, why he told me he was a major. And he paraded around as a major there for two or three days. But when it came down to giving testimony, he admitted he was a first lieutenant. Crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has what you experienced at the liberation affected your attitudes and values?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suppose one thing. It was the most terrible lesson you could ever get on discrimination.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I speak at high schools and other places today, I say discrimination is one of the world's greatest evils, responsible for millions of deaths. And I point out that none of us have a choice of what religion or what race we belong to. None of us volunteered to come into the world so you're stuck with whatever we happen to be, whether you're a Muslim or Catholic or Baptist or black or white. No one could help it, so why should we discriminate against people who had no choice about what their color or race or religion is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think when you see those concentration camps, you see discrimination-- --at the lowest depth of degradation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One thing I've never seen mentioned hardly at all is the fact that there was another group of people who were discriminated against even worse the Jewish people were, and those were the gypsies. The gypsies were rounded up and exterminated offhand in Germany. I mean, they were just-- many of the Jewish people, at least the younger Jewish men, served in labor camps. So thousands of them survived by serving in the labor camps. The policy in Nazi Germany was for women and children to be executed promptly. They were considered worthless. There were no children in Dachau. They were-- and the older people were singled out for extermination. But many in the younger group, say 16 to 30, survived because they were put in labor camps, although younger ones also were killed in the concentration camps at Auschwitz and Buchenwald are two of the worst that come to mind. But the gypsies, they weren't even put in labor camps. They were just killed out of hand. There's nobody to speak for the gypsies, you see. They have no nation, no country so nobody ever hardly mentions the gypsies. But when you look at the Nazi records, that was a group that was discriminated against terribly and executed promptly. They don't go to labor camps or anything. There weren't a great number of them, but nobody knows how many, but it was many thousands. And one religion that I found out in my-- all the books I read about Dachau were discriminated against the same way was Jehovah's Witnesses. Now, that was a very, very small group in Germany, but they were considered in the same light as the gypsies. And probably only a few of them, but they were promptly executed, along with the gypsies. Of course, we have Jehovah's Witnesses in this country. It's a fairly large organization. But it was-- they had a small organization in Germany. The other group, religion, that was discriminated against by the Germans were the Catholics. Catholic priests were simply rounded up. And they were part of the original group that was sent to Dachau starting in 1933. And when they invaded Poland and took over Poland, the Catholic priests, most of them went to Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was another group. But they did not-- it appears no record of actual extermination of the priests. There were some executed, but because they were-- they got rebellious. But-- but even the Lutheran, which Germany is predominantly Lutheran, Pastor Niemoller was one of the leaders of the Lutheran Church in Germany, in Berlin. He finally spoke out against Hitler and he was put in Dachau. Now, he and some of the other important prisoners that were in Dachau were taken out of Dachau several weeks before we arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not there. These were the ones that Margarita Higgins were looking for were not there. We found out they had been moved to Austria before we arrived and the-- Pastor Niemoller survived the war. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I remember he-- in some of his remembrances, I've seen some of the writings, he said something about they persecuted the Jews and I said nothing; they persecuted the labor unions and I said nothing-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --and then they came for me. [LAUGHING] He made an observation something like that. But he did speak out and that's the reason they came for him. But he survived and there apparently was no systematic execution of the-- of priests of various religions, although some were executed in the records for trying to lead a rebellion or speaking out, even when they're in prison camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about homosexuals, did you see any records of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Homosexuals were also discriminated against. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --in our classification of prisoners, we did not try to identify those, if there were any, and I presume there were some. But I know they had a special marking. Each prisoner was supposed to have a special marking on an emblem on his shoulder. The Jewish would have the star of David and the-- oh, various insignia were-- homosexuals had their own insignia. But if there were any in Dachau, I never knew about it. And apparently, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --not an intensive drive, as near as I can gather-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --because Ernst Rohm, who was the head of the Brown Shirts, the organization that put Hitler into power, was a homosexual. Hitler had him killed, but not because he was homosexual, because he wanted him out of the way. He considered Mr. Rohm too powerful. See, Ernst Rohm's Brown Shirts was a civilian organization who were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --were supported by German industrialists, Krapp and so on. They wanted to get rid of the Versailles Treaty and rearm Germany. And Hitler and the Brown Shirts were-- there were having to do that. Well, the Brown Shirts were well equipped. They were in uniform-- they wore a brown uniform. They were well equipped. They were just like an army and they were much larger than the German Army. So when Hitler came to power, the German general says, we-- we're not going to support you. We can't put up with a civilian army. So Hitler agreed to get rid of the Brown Shirts, but he took many of them with him-- with him, of course, like Goebbels and Goring and-- but Ernst Rohm, who was the head of the Brown Shirts, Hitler had executed. In one swoop he took out all the Brown Shirts that he did not like and they were disbanded. But Hitler double-crossed the generals because he turned around and created the SS outside of the German army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever-- you saw many of these records. Were you ever asked to testify at the Nuremberg Trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was. But I refused to go because at that time, I was a district attorney over in western Colorado and I felt that I could not contribute really anything at all because what I saw, thousands of other people saw and had been photographed. And besides, the incident of our killing the German guards would have not looked good on cross-examination.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I refused to go. And of course, they had no jurisdiction over me at all, an American citizen here in this country. The war crimes tribunal had no jurisdiction over me. Were you subpoenaed or just requested to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a subpoena from the United States Marshall.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think we'll end this tape and begin the next one.
